description: Figure 1 comprises two grouped horizontal bar charts that compare room-temperature compressive yield stress (dark grey bars, MPa) and matrix Brinell hardness (light grey bars, HB) for as-cast Fe–Al–M–C alloys. Panel (a) presents Fe–15Al-based alloys including binary Fe–15Al, Fe–15Al–2Ti–1C, Fe–15Al–2V–1C, Fe–15Al–2Nb–1C, and Fe–15Al–2Ta–1C. Panel (b) presents Fe–26Al-based alloys including binary Fe–26Al, Fe–26Al–2Ti–1C, Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C, Fe–26Al–2V–1C, Fe–26Al–2Nb–1C, Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C, and Fe–26Al–2Ta–1C. In the Fe–15Al series, Nb- and Ta-bearing alloys exhibit the highest compressive yield stresses (~820–870 MPa) and hardnesses (~290–310 HB), far exceeding the binary baseline (~350 MPa, ~160 HB). In the Fe–26Al series, most ternary and quaternary additions reduce compressive yield stress below the binary level (~640 MPa), except Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C (~790 MPa). The caption and text report that hardness correlates with compressive yield stress for Ti- and V-containing Fe–15Al alloys, whereas most Fe–26Al-based alloys show no such correlation. The figure supports the conclusion that carbide and/or Laves phase precipitation strongly strengthens Fe–15Al but generally degrades room-temperature compressive yield stress in Fe–26Al, with the high-Nb composition being the notable exception.

This figure presents room-temperature compression engineering stress–strain curves for Fe–15Al-based (a) and Fe–26Al-based (b) alloys, as stated in the caption. Subfigure (a) shows curves for the binary Fe–15Al matrix (curve 1) and variants alloyed with 2Ti–1C (2), 2V–1C (3), 2Nb–1C (4), and 2Ta–1C (5), while subfigure (b) displays the binary Fe–26Al (6) and its 2Ti–1C (7), 4Ti–1C (8), 2V–1C (9), 2Nb–1C (10), 4Nb–1C (11), and 2Ta–1C (12) strengthened counterparts. Solid circles mark the 0.2 % offset yield strength (σ₀.₂) on each curve. Visually, the Fe–15Al-based alloys exhibit pronounced parabolic work hardening after yielding. Citation sentences report that the Fe–26Al–(2 or 4)M–1C alloys show very high strengthening with almost constant hardening rates until a final parabolic work-hardening stage, whereas binary Fe–26Al displays a linear elastic region followed by parabolic hardening. Although most precipitation-hardened Fe–26Al-based alloys possess lower initial yield stresses than the binary alloy, they exhibit substantially stronger hardening during plastic deformation. These curves collectively demonstrate that additions of Ti, V, Nb, or Ta with carbon—which form strengthening MC carbides and/or Laves phases—markedly enhance the compressive yield strength and work-hardening response of Fe–Al intermetallic alloys.

Figure 3 presents room-temperature tensile engineering stress-strain curves for cast Fe-Al-M-C alloys (M = Ti, V, Nb, Ta) strengthened by carbides and/or Laves phase. Subfigure (a) displays five curves for Fe-15Al-based alloys: binary Fe-15Al (1), Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C (2), Fe-15Al-2V-1C (3), Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C (4), and Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C (5). Subfigure (b) displays seven curves for Fe-26Al-based alloys: binary Fe-26Al (6), Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C (7), Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C (8), Fe-26Al-2V-1C (9), Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C (10), Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C (11), and Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C (12). Solid circles indicate the 0.2 % offset yield strength. Binary Fe-15Al shows substantial plastic strain, whereas all precipitate-containing alloys exhibit severely limited tensile ductility, with several specimens fracturing shortly after yielding or within the elastic regime. The Fe-26Al-based alloys are even more brittle, with most curves terminating at very low strain. Nb- and Ta-bearing compositions reach the highest stress levels but display almost no tensile plasticity. Collectively, the data demonstrate that MC carbide and Laves-phase precipitation provide significant strengthening yet drastically degrade room-temperature tensile ductility, consistent with the reported tension-compression asymmetry in these materials.

Figure 4 presents SEM fractographs of Fe–Al–M–C (M = Ti, V, Nb, Ta) alloys after room-temperature tensile testing, with twelve subpanels (a–l) each including a 10 μm scale bar. Subpanel (a) shows the binary Fe–15Al alloy with very fine transgranular cleavage facets. Subpanels (b–e) depict Fe–15Al–2M–1C alloys (M = Ti, V, Nb, Ta, respectively), all exhibiting coarse transgranular cleavage corresponding to brittle behavior. Subpanel (f) shows the binary Fe–26Al alloy with very coarse transgranular fracture. Subpanels (g) and (h) present Fe–26Al–2Ti–1C and Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C, respectively, where cube-like TiC precipitates are visible on partially intergranular fracture areas. Subpanel (i) shows Fe–26Al–2V–1C with uniform steps indicating transgranular fracture influenced by the crystallographic orientation of plate-like VC precipitates. Subpanel (j) shows Fe–26Al–2Nb–1C with very fine facetted transgranular cleavage. Subpanel (k) shows Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C with mixed intergranular decohesion and transgranular cleavage, where densely distributed precipitates create rough decohesion areas. Subpanel (l) shows Fe–26Al–2Ta–1C with mixed fracture characteristics. The figure demonstrates that Fe–15Al-based alloys fail predominantly by transgranular cleavage, whereas Fe–26Al-based alloys exhibit more complex fracture modes, including partial intergranular decohesion influenced by precipitate type and content, correlating with their room-temperature mechanical behavior.

This figure presents the temperature dependency of the compressive yield stress for Fe–15Al-based alloys tested from room temperature up to 800 °C. The plot contains five distinct curves corresponding to the binary Fe-15Al matrix and four ternary variants alloyed with 2 at.% Ti, V, Nb, or Ta plus 1 at.% C, specifically Fe-15Al, Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, and Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C. The vertical axis denotes yield stress in compression in MPa, while the horizontal axis denotes temperature in degrees Celsius. All five alloys exhibit a gradual, monotonous decrease in yield strength as temperature increases. Notably, the Nb- and Ta-containing alloys maintain the highest yield stresses across the entire temperature range, starting near 830 MPa and 860 MPa at room temperature respectively, and remaining significantly above the Ti- and V-modified alloys and the binary Fe-15Al baseline. Above approximately 600 °C, the curves converge more rapidly toward low stress values. The caption and text confirm that precipitation strengthening via MC carbides is effective for all compositions, with NbC and TaC providing superior high-temperature stability and the greatest resistance to strength loss with increasing temperature.

The figure presents the temperature dependence of compressive yield stress (0–800 °C) for Fe-26Al-based alloys, divided into two sub-panels. Panel (a) compares binary Fe-26Al with Ti- and V-containing alloys (Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2V-1C), while panel (b) compares the binary alloy with Nb- and Ta-containing alloys (Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C). All curves exhibit an anomalous yield-strength peak at intermediate temperatures. The Ti- and V-bearing alloys in (a) show a peak near 550 °C at approximately 400 MPa. In (b), the Nb- and Ta alloys display a peak shifted to lower temperatures and higher stresses relative to the binary alloy; Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C reaches the highest peak at 474 °C and 619 MPa. These trends demonstrate that precipitation strengthening by MC carbides and Laves phase (Fe,Al)2Nb markedly enhances high-temperature strength, with the Nb-modified alloy exhibiting the strongest resistance to thermal softening across the tested temperature range.

Figure 7 presents bar charts comparing the yield stresses measured at 650 °C in compression (dark grey bars) and in tension (light grey bars) for cast Fe–Al–M–C alloys. Panel (a) displays data for Fe-15Al-based alloys, including binary Fe-15Al and variants alloyed with 2Ti-1C, 2V-1C, 2Nb-1C, and 2Ta-1C. Panel (b) shows Fe-26Al-based alloys, covering binary Fe-26Al and variants with 2Ti-1C, 4Ti-1C, 2V-1C, 2Nb-1C, 4Nb-1C, and 2Ta-1C. In all investigated alloys, the compressive yield stress visibly exceeds the tensile yield stress, confirming a pronounced tension–compression asymmetry at 650 °C. Additions of Nb and Ta provide the most significant strengthening in both alloy systems; for example, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C and Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C exhibit compressive yield stresses around 430 MPa and 410 MPa, respectively, far surpassing the unalloyed baseline. Similarly, in the Fe-26Al system, the 4Nb-1C variant achieves the highest values, attributed to combined strengthening by NbC carbides and (Fe,Al)2Nb Laves phase. The data collectively demonstrate that carbide and Laves phase precipitation markedly enhance high-temperature strength, yet the tension–compression asymmetry persists across all compositions.

The figure comprises two horizontal bar charts reporting the 800 °C yield stress in compression (dark grey bars) and tension (light grey bars) for cast Fe–Al–M–C alloys. Panel (a) covers Fe–15Al-based alloys—Fe-15Al, Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, and Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C—while panel (b) covers Fe–26Al-based alloys: Fe-26Al, Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2V-1C, Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, and Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C. For every composition, the compressive yield stress exceeds the tensile yield stress, confirming pronounced tension–compression asymmetry at 800 °C. In the Fe–15Al series, Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C shows the highest values, reading approximately 104 MPa in compression and 80 MPa in tension. In the Fe–26Al series, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C reaches roughly 136 MPa and 82 MPa respectively. Overall, Nb-, Ta-, and higher-Ti additions provide the greatest high-temperature strengthening, yet the asymmetric deformation behaviour persists across all investigated alloys.
